topics.title: Stack & Queue
topics.story: A stack is like a stack of plates at a buffet — you always take from the top and put on the top (LIFO). A queue is like people waiting in line at a coffee shop — first in, first out (FIFO). These two structures are the backbone of function calls, browser history, BFS, and undo operations.
topics.analogy: Stack = undo history in VS Code (Ctrl+Z removes last action). Queue = print spooler (documents print in the order they were submitted).
topics.memoryAnchor: STACK: Last In, First Out — like a gun magazine, last bullet in shoots first. QUEUE: First In, First Out — like a fair queue.
topics.firstPrinciples: Stack: push/pop/peek are all O(1). Used when you need LIFO ordering or to 'remember where you came from' (recursion, DFS, expression parsing). Queue: enqueue/dequeue are O(1). Used when FIFO ordering matters (BFS, task processing). Deque supports both.
topics.bruteForce: For balanced parentheses: count opens and closes — fails for mismatched types. For next greater element: nested loops O(n²). For BFS: recursion (wrong — creates DFS order). Always think whether LIFO or FIFO is the right ordering for your problem.
topics.optimizedApproach: Monotonic stack for next greater/smaller element in O(n). Deque for sliding window maximum in O(n). Two-stack trick for queue implementation. Stack to convert recursive DFS to iterative.
topics.whenToUse: Stack: DFS, expression evaluation, balanced parentheses, undo, call stack simulation. Queue: BFS, producer-consumer, rate limiting, task scheduling. Deque: sliding window max/min, palindrome check.

examples.title: Balanced Parentheses Check
examples.explanation: Push open brackets. For close brackets, check if the top matches. Empty stack at end means all balanced.

problems.title: Valid Parentheses
problems.description:
Given a string containing only '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', ']', determine if the input string is valid.

Valid means: open brackets are closed by the same type in the correct order.

**Example:**
Input: "()[]{}" → true
Input: "([)]" → false
Input: "{[]}" → true
problems.hint1: When you see an open bracket, what should you do? When you see a close bracket, what should you check?
problems.hint2: Push open brackets onto the stack. When you see a close bracket, pop and verify it matches the close bracket's corresponding open.
problems.hint3: Three checks: (1) stack empty when you see a close bracket → false, (2) top doesn't match → false, (3) stack not empty at end → false.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ s.length ≤ 10^4 | s consists of parentheses only
problems.editorial: Classic stack problem. O(n) time, O(n) space. The invariant: at every close bracket, the most recent unmatched open bracket must be the corresponding type. Stack naturally enforces this.

problems.title: Min Stack
problems.description:
Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time.

- push(val): push element onto stack
- pop(): remove the top element
- top(): get the top element
- getMin(): retrieve the minimum element in the stack

All operations O(1).
problems.hint1: How can you track the minimum efficiently? Can you store extra information alongside each element?
problems.hint2: Use a second stack that tracks the minimum at each level. When you push val, push min(val, currentMin) onto the min stack.
problems.hint3: Both stacks stay in sync — push to both, pop from both. minStack.peek() is always the current minimum.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"push(-2), push(0), push(-3), getMin(), pop(), top(), getMin()","output":"-3, 0, -2"}]
problems.constraints: -2^31 ≤ val ≤ 2^31 - 1 | At most 3*10^4 calls | pop/top/getMin called only on non-empty stack
problems.editorial: Auxiliary min stack stores the running minimum at every level. Both stacks are always the same size. O(1) all operations, O(n) space. Alternative: store (val, minAtThisLevel) as pairs in one stack.

problems.title: Daily Temperatures
problems.description:
Given an array of integers temperatures, return an array answer where answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day with a warmer temperature, answer[i] = 0.

**Example:**
Input: [73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73]
Output: [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0]
problems.hint1: For each day, you need to find the nearest future day with a higher temperature. Brute force is O(n²). Can you do O(n)?
problems.hint2: Use a stack that stores indices of temperatures we haven't found a warmer day for yet. When does a waiting day get resolved?
problems.hint3: Maintain a monotonically decreasing stack of indices. When you see temperatures[i] > temperatures[stack.top()], the top index found its warmer day. answer[popped] = i - popped.
problems.editorial: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. Each element is pushed once and popped once → O(n) amortized. When popped at index i, the wait = i - popped_index. Remaining stack elements have answer 0 (default array values).

problems.title: Implement Queue Using Two Stacks
problems.description:
Implement a FIFO queue using only two stacks. Supported operations: push(x), pop(), peek(), empty().

**Example:**
push(1), push(2), peek() → 1, pop() → 1, empty() → false
problems.hint1: Stack is LIFO, queue is FIFO. How can two stacks give you FIFO ordering?
problems.hint2: One stack for pushing (inbox), one for popping (outbox). When the outbox is empty and you need to dequeue, pour all inbox elements into outbox — this reverses the order to FIFO.
problems.hint3: Amortized O(1): each element is moved from inbox to outbox at most once. Only move when outbox is empty.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ x ≤ 9 | At most 100 calls to push, pop, peek, empty | Always valid (pop/peek on non-empty)
problems.editorial: Two-stack queue: inbox for writes, outbox for reads. Lazy transfer on read (only when outbox empty). Each element transferred at most once → amortized O(1) per operation. O(n) space.

problems.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
problems.description:
Given an array of integers heights representing the heights of bars in a histogram (width = 1 each), find the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.

**Example:**
Input: [2,1,5,6,2,3]
Output: 10  (rectangle of height 5, width 2, between index 2–3)
problems.hint1: For each bar, the rectangle extends as far left and right as bars of equal or greater height. How do you find those boundaries efficiently?
problems.hint2: Use a monotonic increasing stack of indices. When you find a shorter bar, pop — the popped bar is the height, and the boundaries are determined by the current index and the new stack top.
problems.hint3: For each popped index h: height = heights[h], right_boundary = current_i, left_boundary = stack.isEmpty() ? 0 : stack.peek() + 1. Area = height * (right - left). Add sentinel 0 at end to flush all bars.
problems.editorial: Monotonic increasing stack. Key insight: for each bar as the height of a rectangle, extend as far as shorter bars allow. The stack gives us the left and right boundaries in O(1) per bar. O(n) time, O(n) space. Sentinel 0 forces the stack to flush at the end.

problems.title: Sliding Window Maximum
problems.description:
Given an integer array nums and a window size k, return the maximum of each window as it slides from left to right.

**Example:**
Input: nums = [1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], k = 3
Output: [3,3,5,5,6,7]
problems.hint1: Brute force checks every window in O(k) → total O(nk). Can you maintain the maximum as you slide in O(1) per step?
problems.hint2: Use a deque (double-ended queue) to maintain a monotonically decreasing window of indices. The front is always the current maximum.
problems.hint3: For each new element i: (1) remove from back any indices with smaller values — they can never be the max while i is in the window. (2) Remove from front any indices outside the window (j <= i - k). (3) Front of deque = max of current window.
problems.editorial: Monotonic deque stores indices in decreasing order of values. Front = max of current window. Each element enqueued and dequeued at most once → O(n) total. Key: maintain window validity (remove front if out of range) AND maintain monotonicity (remove back if smaller than new element).

problems.title: Next Greater Element I
problems.description:
Given two arrays nums1 and nums2 (nums1 is a subset of nums2), find for each element in nums1 its next greater element in nums2. The next greater element of x is the first element to the right of x in nums2 that is greater than x. Return -1 if none.

**Example:**
nums1=[4,1,2], nums2=[1,3,4,2]
Output: [-1,3,-1] (4 has no greater, 1's next greater is 3, 2 has no greater)
problems.hint1: For each element in nums2, find its next greater element. Then answer queries from nums1 using a lookup.
problems.hint2: Process nums2 with a monotonic stack. When you find the next greater for element x, store it in a HashMap: x → nextGreater(x).
problems.hint3: For each element in nums1, look up the HashMap. If not present (means it's the maximum in its suffix), return -1.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ nums1.length ≤ nums2.length ≤ 1000 | 0 ≤ nums1[i], nums2[i] ≤ 10^4 | All elements unique
problems.editorial: Precompute NGE for all nums2 elements in O(n) using monotonic stack. Store in HashMap. Answer all nums1 queries in O(m) using lookup. Total O(m+n) vs O(m*n) brute force.

problems.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
problems.description:
Evaluate the value of an arithmetic expression in Reverse Polish Notation (postfix). Valid operators: +, -, *, /. Integer division truncates toward zero.

**Example:**
Tokens: ['2','1','+','3','*'] → Output: 9 ((2+1)*3)
Tokens: ['4','13','5','/','+'] → Output: 6 (4+(13/5))
problems.hint1: In RPN, operators come after their operands. When you see an operator, apply it to the last two numbers seen. What data structure maintains the last seen numbers?
problems.hint2: Use a stack. For each token: if number → push. If operator → pop two, apply operator, push result.
problems.hint3: Pop order matters: first pop = right operand, second pop = left operand. So: b = pop(), a = pop(), push(a op b).
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ tokens.length ≤ 10^4 | Each token is an integer or one of +, -, *, / | Input is always a valid RPN expression | Result fits in 32-bit int
problems.editorial: Classic stack application. RPN was invented specifically so expressions can be evaluated with a single stack pass in O(n). Pop order: second pop is left operand, first pop is right operand (b = pop, a = pop, push a op b).

problems.title: Design Circular Queue
problems.description:
Implement a circular queue (ring buffer) with fixed capacity k.
- enQueue(val): inserts an element, returns false if full
- deQueue(): deletes the front element, returns false if empty
- Front(): gets the front item, -1 if empty
- Rear(): gets the last item, -1 if empty
- isEmpty(), isFull()

All operations O(1).
problems.hint1: Use an array of size k with two pointers: head (front) and tail (insertion point). How do you wrap around when you reach the end of the array?
problems.hint2: Use modulo arithmetic: tail = (tail + 1) % k when enqueuing. head = (head + 1) % k when dequeuing. Track size to distinguish full from empty.
problems.hint3: Size variable avoids the ambiguity of head==tail meaning full OR empty. Alternatively, use (tail+1)%k == head to detect full.
problems.testCases: [{"input":"MyCircularQueue(3): enQueue(1), enQueue(2), enQueue(3), enQueue(4), Rear(), isFull(), deQueue(), enQueue(4), Rear()","output":"true, true, true, false, 3, true, true, true, 4"}]
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ k ≤ 1000 | 0 ≤ value ≤ 1000 | At most 3000 calls to each operation
problems.editorial: Ring buffer with modulo arithmetic. size variable cleanly tracks count. Rear = (tail-1+k)%k to get last inserted element. Used in: OS I/O buffers, producer-consumer queues, audio streaming ring buffers.

problems.title: Implement Stack Using Queues
problems.description:
Implement a LIFO stack using only queue operations. Supported: push(x), pop(), top(), empty().

**Example:**
push(1), push(2), top()→2, pop()→2, empty()→false
problems.hint1: A queue is FIFO. How can you make the newest element accessible from the front?
problems.hint2: After pushing element x: rotate the queue so x moves to the front. Enqueue x, then dequeue and re-enqueue all previous elements (size-1 times).
problems.hint3: After rotation, the newly pushed element is at the front — pop() and top() work naturally from the front.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ x ≤ 9 | At most 100 calls | Always valid (pop/top on non-empty)
problems.editorial: After each push, rotate queue so new element is at front. O(n) push, O(1) pop/top. Symmetric to Queue-with-2-Stacks. Rotation trick: enqueue x, then dequeue-and-reenqueue all n-1 previous elements.
